entry.title: SAP Fiori Elements List Report: Implementing Dual Data Views with Flat and Hierarchical Entities
entry.content:
In many SAP Fiori Elements applications, data is modeled hierarchically. Consider a scenario with a 3-level entity structure:

  • Header (parent)
  • Item (child)
  • SubItem (grandchild)

By default, the List Report displays data at the Header level. However, there is a requirement to provide a Detailed view: a flat representation that surfaces fields from all three levels in a single row, without requiring navigation into each record.

Goal:

Segmented button on the List Report that lets the user toggle between:

  • Detailed View: flat structure, fields from all 3 levels in one row
  • Normal View: standard hierarchical view at the Header level

Why this approach

Instead of modifying the existing hierarchical entity, a separate flat view entity allows both representations to coexist without impacting the original data model.

 

Backend: Exposing a Flat View Entity (CAP)

The standard Header entity only holds parent-level fields. To surface child and grandchild fields in a single flat list, a dedicated view entity is exposed in the CAP service.

This entity (HeaderDetailView) is built using a bottom-to-top approach, starting from SubItem and joining upward through Item to Header, making all fields available at a single level.

// In schema.cds

entity HeaderDetailView as select from SubItem {
    // SubItem fields
    subItemField1 as field1,
    subItemField2 as field2,

    // Item fields (via association)
    item.itemField1 as field3
    item.itemField2 as field4

    // Header fields (via association)
    item.header.headerField1 as field5,
    item.header.headerField2 as field6,
    item.header.ID as ID
};

This entity is then exposed in the service:

// In service.cds

service AppService {
    entity Header         as projection on db.Header;
    entity HeaderDetailView as projection on db.HeaderDetailView;
}

 

UI: Wiring Two Entities into One List Report

The UI layer uses annotations and manifest configuration to expose both entities as separate views within the same List Report.

Step 1: Define LineItem annotations for both views

Detailed view: on HeaderDetailView:

//In Annotations.cds file

annotate service.HeaderDetailView with @(UI: { 
    HeaderInfo                       : {
        TypeName      : 'Item',
        TypeNamePlural: 'Items'
    },
    PresentationVarinat #DetailedView: {
        Visualizations: [{$AnnotationPath:'HeaderDetailView/@UI.LineItem#DetailedView'}],
        GroupBy       : [ID],
    },
    PresentationVarinat #NormalView  : {
        Visualizations: [{$AnnotationPath:'Header/@UI.LineItem'}],
    },
    LineItem #DetailedView           : [
        {
            $Type            : 'UI.DataField',
            Value            : ID,
            ![@UI.Importance]: #High
        },
        {
            $Type            : 'UI.DataField',
            Value            : field1,
            ![@UI.Importance]: #High
        },
        //add other fields in similar fashion
    ],
});

Normal view: on Header:

//In Annotations.cds file

annotate service.Header with @(UI: { 
    HeaderInfo                       : {
        TypeName      : 'Item',
        TypeNamePlural: 'Items'
    },
    LineItem          : [
        {
            $Type            : 'UI.DataField',
            Value            : ID,
            ![@UI.Importance]: #High
        },
        {
            $Type            : 'UI.DataField',
            Value            : field1,
            ![@UI.Importance]: #High
        },
        //add other header level-fields in similar fashion
    ],
});

 

Step 2: Define SelectionPresentationVariant on both entities

This is what the List Report uses to derive the tab label and resolve the correct LineItem.

//In Annotations.cds file

annotate service.HeaderDetailView with @(UI: {
    SelectionPresentationVariant #DetailedView: {
        $Type              : 'UI.SelectionPresentationVariantType',
        Text               : 'Detailed View',
        PresentationVariant: {
            Visualizations: ['@UI.LineItem#DetailedView']
        }
    }
});

annotate service.Header with @(UI: { 
    SelectionPresentationVariant #NormalView: {
        $Type              : 'UI.SelectionPresentationVariantType',
        Text               : 'Normal View',
        PresentationVariant: {
            Visualizations: ['@UI.LineItem']
        }
    }
});

 

Step 3: Configure manifest.json

"targets": {
        "HeaderList": {
          "type": "Component",
          "id": "HeaderList",
          "name": "sap.fe.templates.ListReport",
          "options": {
            "settings": {
              "contextPath": "/HeaderDetailView",
              "views": {
                "paths": [
                  {
                    "key": "tab1",
                    "annotationPath": "com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionPresentationVariant#DetailedView"
                  },
                  {
                    "key": "tab2",
                    "entitySet": "ObjectPageEntity",
                    "annotationPath": "com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionPresentationVariant#NormalView"
                  }
                ]

              },
              
              "enhanceI18n": "i18n/i18n.properties",
              "variantManagement": "Page",
              "initialLoad": "Enabled",
              "navigation": {
                "ObjectPageEntity": {
                  "detail": {
                    "route": "ObjectPageEntityRoute"
                  }
                }
              },
              "controlConfiguration": {
                "@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionPresentationVariant#DetailedView": {
                  "tableSettings": {
                    "type": "ResponsiveTable",
                    "disableCopyToClipboard": true,
                    "enablePaste": false,
                    "personalization": {
                      "column": true,
                      "sort": true,
                      "filter": true,
                      "group": true
                    }
                  },
                  "columns": {
                  }
                },
                "/Header/@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.SelectionPresentationVariant#NormalView": {
                  "tableSettings": {
                    "type": "ResponsiveTable",
                    "disableCopyToClipboard": true,
                    "enablePaste": false,
                    "personalization": {
                      "filter": true,
                      "group":false
                    }
                  },
                  "columns": {
                 }
           }
        },

This configuration enables the List Report to render multiple views backed by different entity sets.

Key points:

  • contextPath points to the flat view entity (HeaderDetailView) as the primary entity
  • Tab2 uses entitySet to point to the hierarchical entity (Header)
  • annotationPath must point to SelectionPresentationVariant (not LineItem), as it provides both the visualization reference and the tab label.

Result

The List Report renders a segmented button on the table toolbar, allowing users to switch between Detailed View and Normal View. Thus, both views are available at the same List Report level, without requiring navigation.

>> Normal View:

>> Detailed View:

Summary

Comparison of Views:

 

Detailed View

Normal View

Entity

HeaderDetailView (flat)

Header (hierarchical)

Structure

Fields from all 3 levels

Parent-level fields only

Annotation

UI.LineItem#DetailedView

UI.LineItem

Variant

SelectionPresentationVariant#DetailedView

SelectionPresentationVariant#NormalView

 

Final takeaway

SAP Fiori Elements List Report supports multiple views backed by different entity sets, not just filtered variants of the same entity. SelectionPresentationVariant acts as the bridge between CDS annotations and manifest configuration, controlling both the tab label and the rendered content.

When to use this approach

  • When a hierarchical model needs a flattened analytical view
  • When users need detailed data without navigating to Object Pages
  • When multiple representations of the same data are required in a single List Report

entry.title: OData V4 Fiori Elements: "KeyPath missing at p.getItemFromContext" when selecting Value Help - Fiori
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

I am facing a persistent UI5 ERROR in an OData V4 Fiori Elements application (based on ABAP RAP).

The Scenario: I have a Root Consumption View with an input Parameter. I have assigned a custom CDS Value Help (Dropdown / #XS) to this parameter. The dropdown successfully opens and displays the data in the Fiori Preview.

The Error: When the user clicks/selects an item from the dropdown list, the slection doesn't happen with the following console error in the browser:

Uncaught (in promise) Error: KeyPath missing at p.getItemFromContext

1. Value Help CDS View (Single Key, Dropdown)

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Value Help for Parameter'
@ObjectModel.resultSet.sizeCategory: #XS  

define view entity ZI_VH_CustomValue
  as select from custom_domain_table
{
      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
        key cast( domain_value as abap.char(1) ) as ValueKey,

      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
      @Semantics.text: true
      domain_text as Description
}
where active_status = 'A'

2. The Consumption View (Short Version)

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Main Business Object'
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true

define root view entity ZC_MainBusinessObject
  with parameters

    @EndUserText.label: 'Custom Filter Parameter'
    @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
        entity: { name: 'ZI_VH_CustomValue', element: 'ValueKey' },

        /* Explicit binding to pass the value back to the parameter */
        additionalBinding: [{
            localParameter: 'p_custom_filter',
            element: 'ValueKey',
            usage: #RESULT
        }]
    }]
    p_custom_filter : z_custom_data_element

  as select from ZI_MainBusinessObject
{
  key DocumentId,
      DocumentType,
      PostingDate
}
where PostingDate <= $session.system_date

My Questions:

  1. Is there a known limitation or missing @ui/ @Consumption annotation required to bind a Value Help strictly to a PARAMETER (rather than a standard table field) in OData V4?
  2. Why is the OData V4 framework failing to resolve the context for the selected item despite the explicit localParameter binding?

Any insights or workarounds would be highly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Managing Content Change Notification Issues During SAP Fiori Launchpad Content Exposure to SAP BTP
entry.content:
With the SAP Fiori launchpad content exposure, you can integrate launchpad content from a product into SAP BTP by using an automatic synchronization. During the automatic synchronization, it may happen that the exposure log receives the following error message “Notification for content channel ‘xyz’ not sent. Manual fetch required” (Message Class: /UI2/CDM3_EXP, Message Number: 399).

As you may not immediately recognize that this issue happened, this blog post provides a way of how you can monitor this type of error message using email notification from SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (SAP Cloud ALM).

In short, in the SAP Cloud ALM Integration & Exception Monitoring app, you do the following:

  1. Enable the ABAP application log for the system          
  2. Add a filter for the error message
  3. Add a notification for the error

As an example, we use an SAP S/4HANA system.

Disclaimer: This article is written from the viewpoint of a consumer of SAP Cloud ALM. If you have SAP Cloud ALM-specific questions, such as how to set up your system in SAP Cloud ALM, please reach out to SAP Cloud ALM directly.

Check the following SAP Help Portal pages to see whether you are affected by this issue:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

  • Enable Content Change Notifications
  • Display Launchpad Content Exposure Logs

SAP Integrated Business Planning

  • Enable Content Change Notifications
  • Display Launchpad Content Exposure Logs

SAP S/4HANA on-premise

  • Manage Content Change Notifications
  • Launchpad Content Exposure Troubleshooting - Error Analysis   

Prerequisites

Before we can start with the parts specific to launchpad exposure in the SAP Cloud ALM configuration, you set up your system in SAP Cloud ALM.

In addition, please ensure that you have the Integration Architect role. See SAP Cloud ALM overview page.

Enabling ABAP Application Log for Your System

First, you configure your system to collect data for the ABAP Application Log.

  1. In SAP Cloud ALM, in the Operations space, open the Integration & Exception Monitoring app.
  2. Now, let’s add a system tile to the overview: In the upper right corner, choose the Select a Scope icon and filter for your system. Select it in the table and choose Apply.
  3. On the overview page, choose the system tile and from its menu, choose Edit Configuration.
  4. For your system, toggle the Data Collection to On.
  5. For your system, choose the arrow icon to go to the details page.
  6. Activate the ABAP Application Log entry.

Adding a Filter for the Launchpad Content Exposure Error Message

Now, let’s create a filter so that we can monitor when the error message “Notification for content channel ‘xyz’ not sent. Manual fetch required” is written to the ABAP Application Log.

  1. In the same screen as above, for the ABAP Application Log, enter the monitoring and events view.
  2. To add the filter, in the Filters table, choose Add.
  3. Enter a filter name. Then, enter the following filter parameters:

    Parameter

    Operator

    Value

    Object

    Is

    /UI2/BE

    Subobject

    Is

    /UI2/CDM3

    Message ID

    Is

    /UI2/CDM3_EXP

    Message Number

    Is

    399

    Status

    Is

    E


  4. Save the filter by choosing the Save button at the bottom of the dialog.

Adding an Event to Be Notified About an Error

The last step is to create an event so that you get notified once the filter which we have created in the previous step detects the launchpad content exposure error message.

Please note that this event will not only be initiated for the launchpad content exposure error message. If you had configured other ABAP Application Log filters in addition, the event will be initiated as well. The reason is that on the date of publication of this blog post, according to 3452860 - How to setup filters for the category "ABAP application log" in Exception Monitoring of SAP Cloud ALM, it is not possible to add filters to the event itself.

  1. To create the event, navigate to the Events tab and choose Add.
  2. As Event Name, choose Erroneous ABAP Application Log and enter a display name.
  3. Define the actions: Activate Create Alert. For example, you can also configure the event to send an email once there is an error written into the ABAP Application Log.
     
  4. Save your entries.

Checking the Event

In the Integration & Exception Monitoring app, you can view all events that you have created and that were initiated on the Alerting tab.

  1. Choose the Alerting tab.
  2. Once the launchpad content exposure error happens, you can see it in the table

Conclusion:

This blog post covered how to proactively monitor HTTP 401 content change notification failures during SAP Fiori launchpad content exposure to SAP BTP using SAP Cloud ALM's Integration & Exception Monitoring app. You have learned how to enable ABAP Application Log data collection, create a targeted filter for message /UI2/CDM3_EXP 399, and configure automated email notifications to detect synchronization issues before they impact your users. By implementing this monitoring approach, you can transform reactive troubleshooting into proactive incident management, ensuring continuous content availability between your on-premise systems and SAP BTP.

If you have questions about the configuration steps or would like to share your experiences with launchpad content exposure monitoring, please feel free to leave a comment below.

entry.title: Beginner Guide to SAPUI5: What You Need to Know Before Building Your First App
entry.content:
Hi community, I am a fresher who have been recently onboarded in the SAP UI5 domain. 

It's been a week since I started exploring UI5. In this first week of learning SAPUI5, I focused on understanding the basics that form the foundation of any UI5 application.

In this blog, I want to share what I explored during my first week, what I understood, and how these concepts helped me get more comfortable with SAPUI5 development so that it might help someone who is starting their career in UI5. 

My focus was mainly on understanding the basics — the architecture, environment setup, project structure, simple controls, layouts, and some commonly used complex controls. This week gave me a solid starting point, and I feel much more confident about continuing my learning journey.   Understanding the UI5 Architecture: MVC Pattern

The first concept I explored was the MVC pattern, which is one of the core foundations of SAPUI5 applications.

MVC stands for:

Model View Controller

The View is where the user interface is defined. In SAPUI5, this is often done using XML views.
The Controller handles the logic behind the view — for example, what should happen when a button is clicked.
The Model is responsible for the data that the application works with.

What I found useful about MVC is the way it separates different responsibilities. Instead of keeping everything in one file, SAPUI5 organizes the application in a cleaner and more manageable way. For a beginner, understanding this pattern was very important because it helped me see how different parts of the application connect with each other.

Once I became comfortable with MVC, the overall structure of a UI5 app started looking less confusing.

Setting Up the Development Environment

The next thing I explored was the development environment. 

SAP Business Application Studio SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio is designed with SAP development in mind, so it provides a more guided and integrated experience. It felt like a good option for building SAPUI5 or SAP Fiori applications in a more structured way.

Getting Familiar with the Project Structure

One of the most useful things I learned during this week was the project structure of a SAPUI5 application.

In the beginning, when I saw multiple folders and files, it was a bit difficult to understand which file does what. But once I started learning the purpose of each part, the structure began to feel logical.

Some of the key parts I came across were:

webapp – the main application folder view – where XML views are stored controller – where controller files are written model – used for model-related files manifest.json – the application descriptor Component.js – important for bootstrapping and configuration index.html – the starting point of the application

Understanding the project structure made a big difference for me. It became easier to navigate the application, find files, and understand how the app is launched. For someone new to SAPUI5, I think this is one of the most important areas to understand early, because every other topic becomes easier after that.

Building My First UI5 Application

After learning the basics of architecture and project structure, I moved on to creating my first UI5 application.

This was one of the most interesting parts of my first week because it gave me the chance to apply what I had learned. Even though the application was simple, the process taught me a lot.

I started understanding things like:

how a UI5 app is initialized, how the view connects to the controller, how controls are added to the page, and how user actions are handled.

What I liked most about building the first app was that it turned abstract concepts into something practical. Instead of only reading about views and controllers, I could actually see them working together. That made the learning experience much more meaningful.

Exploring Basic Controls

Once the first app was working, I spent time learning some basic controls in SAPUI5. These included:

Button Input Label Text

These controls may seem simple, but they are the foundation of almost every user interface.

Button

The Button control helped me understand how events are handled in SAPUI5. For example, when a user clicks a button, the action is handled in the controller.

Input

The Input control is used to take user input. It showed me how forms and interactive fields are built in UI5 applications.

Label

The Label control is useful for identifying fields clearly. It plays an important role in making the UI readable and user-friendly.

Text

The Text control is used to display information on the screen. It is simple, but very commonly used in applications.

Working with these basic controls helped me become more comfortable with writing XML views. I also started noticing how each control comes with its own properties and purpose.

Learning Layouts: VBox, HBox, and Grid

After understanding controls, I explored how to organize them properly on the screen using layouts.

A UI may have all the right controls, but without a proper layout, it can still look messy. That is why learning layouts felt like an important step.

VBox

The VBox layout arranges controls vertically, one below another. It is useful when the UI needs a top-to-bottom flow.

HBox

The HBox layout arranges controls horizontally. This is useful when elements need to appear side by side in the same row.

Grid

The Grid layout gives more flexibility in arranging controls and is useful for creating more structured and responsive screens.

This part of my learning helped me understand that UI design is not just about adding controls, but also about placing them in a meaningful and organized way. Layouts are essential for creating clean and usable interfaces.

Exploring Complex Controls: Table, List, and Form

Toward the later part of the week, I started looking at some more advanced or complex controls, including:

Table List Form

These controls gave me a better idea of how SAPUI5 supports real business application requirements.

Table

The Table control is useful when data needs to be displayed in a structured row-and-column format. It is especially important in enterprise applications where users work with large sets of business data.

List

The List control is simpler than a table and works well when items need to be shown in an easy-to-read format.

Form

The Form control is useful for structuring input fields, labels, and other form-related content in a clean way. It helps in building screens where users need to enter or edit information.

Learning these controls helped me see the practical side of SAPUI5 more clearly. Until this point, I was looking at UI5 mostly as a framework with views and controllers. But after exploring tables, lists, and forms, I started seeing how it can be used to build real business-ready applications.

What I Took Away from This First Week

Looking back at my first week with SAPUI5, I feel that I covered some very important foundational topics.

The biggest things I understood were:

the importance of the MVC pattern, the role of a proper development environment, the value of understanding the project structure, how basic controls are used in views, why layouts matter in UI design, and how complex controls support enterprise application needs.

More than anything, this week helped me move from confusion to clarity. I may still be at the beginning of my SAPUI5 journey, but I now feel like I have a base to build on.

Conclusion

My first week of learning SAPUI5 was a really valuable experience. I started with the basics, explored the architecture, set up the environment, understood the project structure, built my first app, and worked with different controls and layouts.

There is still a lot more to learn, but this first step gave me confidence. SAPUI5 seems like a structured and practical framework, especially for building enterprise applications, and I am looking forward to exploring it further.

Thanks for reading. I would be happy to know how your first experience with SAPUI5 was and what topics helped you the most in the beginning. 

I am open for any suggestions and guidance of what are the concepts that I need to focus on for the upcoming week and also any resources that might help me going further.

entry.title: Create New Catalog, Space, Page, Section and Role – S4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Like my previous article on classic concept of Fiori Groups in combination with Catalogs, this article is based on single-functional consultant approach where FICO consultants don't have to depend on ABAP or BASIS consultants to achieve overall objective.

We are going to develop a unified Fiori Space, create its relevant page, add related section therein, and then finally assign the respective Fiori tiles / apps.

For smoother learning and easier follow-up, I have devided this article in five main areas:

A. Create New Transport Request 

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

So lets start;

A. Create New Transport Request

Go to t-code SE10 and create a new TR where all configuration activities will be saved.

Click  button

Chose the radio button “Customizing Request”

New Transport Request will be created.

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

  1. In GUI, enter t-code /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST

Following window will open in the browser, this is SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer.

  1. We will create catalog for GL Report F2217 (Display Line Items in General Ledger)

Go to SAP online Fiori Library https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#

and find the app F2217

Go to Tab  and expand the node ‘Configuration’

Find the catalog (either technical or business),

I chose the technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

  1. Go back to FLP Designer page (shown in step B-1).

Click on button available on right top corner of FLP Designer screen.

Following pop-up window will appear, here select the Transport Request you created in step A.

Click OK button to close the pop-up window. Now all activities will save in this transport request.

  1. Click on button available on left bottom of the screen.

A pop-up window will appear, here provide the Title and ID of new catalog.

Click on save button to create our new catalog.

  1. Now in catalog search field, specify the standard technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

Here find the app F2217

Click on the button “Tiles” as shown below:

Tile details will appear in table as below:

Select the row and you will notice few buttons have been enabled in bottom tray.

One of these isclick it, and following pop-up window will show:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of desired tile F2217 in our new catalog.

  1. Now on same screen, select the button “Target Mapping”

Here select the appropriate Semantic Object “GLAccount” and Action “displayGLLineItemReportingView” as shown below:

Now click on buttonand following pop-up window will appear:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of Target Mapping in our new catalog.

Now specify the new Catalog ID in search field (as shown below):

Click on Tile and Target Mapping button to confirm the assignment of same semantic object and action.

This confirms the Tile mapping and Target mapping.

We have completed the creation of new Catalog and it’s mapping with the required app F2217.

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

  1. Go to t-code PFCG and create new role ZFI_GL_REPORTS by clicking the button

     

  2. Click on tab Select Transaction -> SAP Fiori Launchpad -> Launchpad Catalog

     

Following pop-up window will appear,

Here specify the created new catalog ZFI_GL_REPORTS to assign with the role.

Save the data and notice the new catalog assignment.

This completes the catalog assignment to the role.

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

  1. Create New Space

On Fiori, access the app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”

Click on button

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Space ID, Short description, Space title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton

On following screen, as shown below, click on button.

Space is now created and saved.

  1. Create New Page

Access the Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Following screen will show:

Here click on button.

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Page ID, Short description, Page title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton to create the Page.

Following screen will show:

  1. Create New Section

On Page screen, click on  button.

Following field will appear:

Specify the Section title

Click on button to save the section.

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

  1. In GUI, go to t-code PFCG and select the role ZFI_GL_REPORTS, we already created in step C-1.

Click on button

Go to tab, and access the path as shown below:

On following pop-up window, assign the Space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Now click on save button and Space will be assigned to the role.

  1. Assign Page to Space

In Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”, select the already created space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Now click on  button

List of available pages will show.

Here find our page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Select this page by clicking checkbox and then click on button

Click on button.

Page is now assigned to Space.

  1. Assign Fiori App / Tile to Section

Access the Fiori App “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Select the page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Following screen will appear:

Select the Fiori app “Display Line Items in General Ledger”

and click on  button

Now this Fiori tile will show as assigned to page (as shown below)

Click on  button.

This completes the Fiori tile assignment to Page and section.

Now the required app is visible for user on Fiori interface.

Now you can see the tile under the Space and Page.

Just go to User Homepage and find the Space “GL Reports”

Here you can see the Page and Tile under the same Space.

This concludes the article to buidup the Space, Page, Section and Tile/App assignment.

Hope you have enjoyed the read.

Please do comment for any observations and recommendations.

Best Regards,

entry.title: Create New Catalog, Space, Page, Section and Role – S4HANA Private Cloud
entry.content:
Like my previous article on classic concept of Fiori Groups in combination with Catalogs, this article is based on single-functional consultant approach where FICO consultants don't have to depend on ABAP or BASIS consultants to achieve overall objective.

We are going to develop a unified Fiori Space, create its relevant page, add related section therein, and then finally assign the respective Fiori tiles / apps.

For smoother learning and easier follow-up, I have devided this article in five main areas:

A. Create New Transport Request 

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

So lets start;

A. Create New Transport Request

Go to t-code SE10 and create a new TR where all configuration activities will be saved.

Click  button

Chose the radio button “Customizing Request”

New Transport Request will be created.

B. Create New Z Catalog and map it with required Fiori Apps

  1. In GUI, enter t-code /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST

Following window will open in the browser, this is SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer.

  1. We will create catalog for GL Report F2217 (Display Line Items in General Ledger)

Go to SAP online Fiori Library https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#

and find the app F2217

Go to Tab  and expand the node ‘Configuration’

Find the catalog (either technical or business),

I chose the technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

  1. Go back to FLP Designer page (shown in step B-1).

Click on button available on right top corner of FLP Designer screen.

Following pop-up window will appear, here select the Transport Request you created in step A.

Click OK button to close the pop-up window. Now all activities will save in this transport request.

  1. Click on button available on left bottom of the screen.

A pop-up window will appear, here provide the Title and ID of new catalog.

Click on save button to create our new catalog.

  1. Now in catalog search field, specify the standard technical catalog SAP_TC_FIN_ACC_COMMON

Here find the app F2217

Click on the button “Tiles” as shown below:

Tile details will appear in table as below:

Select the row and you will notice few buttons have been enabled in bottom tray.

One of these isclick it, and following pop-up window will show:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of desired tile F2217 in our new catalog.

  1. Now on same screen, select the button “Target Mapping”

Here select the appropriate Semantic Object “GLAccount” and Action “displayGLLineItemReportingView” as shown below:

Now click on buttonand following pop-up window will appear:

Here provide the new catalog ID which we created in step B-4,

Select the catalog and SAP will save the reference of Target Mapping in our new catalog.

Now specify the new Catalog ID in search field (as shown below):

Click on Tile and Target Mapping button to confirm the assignment of same semantic object and action.

This confirms the Tile mapping and Target mapping.

We have completed the creation of new Catalog and it’s mapping with the required app F2217.

C. Create New Role and Assign the New Catalog

  1. Go to t-code PFCG and create new role ZFI_GL_REPORTS by clicking the button

     

  2. Click on tab Select Transaction -> SAP Fiori Launchpad -> Launchpad Catalog

     

Following pop-up window will appear,

Here specify the created new catalog ZFI_GL_REPORTS to assign with the role.

Save the data and notice the new catalog assignment.

This completes the catalog assignment to the role.

D. Create New Space, New Page and New Section

  1. Create New Space

On Fiori, access the app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”

Click on button

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Space ID, Short description, Space title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton

On following screen, as shown below, click on button.

Space is now created and saved.

  1. Create New Page

Access the Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Following screen will show:

Here click on button.

Following pop-up window will appear, here enter the Page ID, Short description, Page title and specify the TR where space data will be saved.

Click onbutton to create the Page.

Following screen will show:

  1. Create New Section

On Page screen, click on  button.

Following field will appear:

Specify the Section title

Click on button to save the section.

E. Assign Fiori Apps to Space, Pages and Sections

  1. In GUI, go to t-code PFCG and select the role ZFI_GL_REPORTS, we already created in step C-1.

Click on button

Go to tab, and access the path as shown below:

On following pop-up window, assign the Space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Now click on save button and Space will be assigned to the role.

  1. Assign Page to Space

In Fiori app “Manage Launchpad Spaces”, select the already created space ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Now click on  button

List of available pages will show.

Here find our page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Select this page by clicking checkbox and then click on button

Click on button.

Page is now assigned to Space.

  1. Assign Fiori App / Tile to Section

Access the Fiori App “Manage Launchpad Pages”

Select the page ZFI_GL_REPORTS

Click on  button

Following screen will appear:

Select the Fiori app “Display Line Items in General Ledger”

and click on  button

Now this Fiori tile will show as assigned to page (as shown below)

Click on  button.

This completes the Fiori tile assignment to Page and section.

Now the required app is visible for user on Fiori interface.

Now you can see the tile under the Space and Page.

Just go to User Homepage and find the Space “GL Reports”

Here you can see the Page and Tile under the same Space.

This concludes the article to buidup the Space, Page, Section and Tile/App assignment.

Hope you have enjoyed the read.

Please do comment for any observations and recommendations.

Best Regards,

entry.title: Introducing the new SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library
entry.content:
The SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library has long been the go-to resource for anyone looking to plan and implement SAP Fiori applications. It provides a comprehensive catalog of all available apps, technical configuration details, installation requirements, and direct links to related resources like the Product Availability Matrix and Maintenance Planner.

We are now releasing a new version, rebuilt with a modern, intuitive interface that dramatically reduces the time it takes to find exactly what you need. More robust filtering lets you find adjacent apps so you can ensure an end-to-end SAP Fiori experience across your business processes.

Live now at fal.cloud.sap , built from the ground up with a focus on clarity and productivity, this new version transforms how SAP customers, functional consultants, partners, administrators and developers explore and implement SAP Fiori apps.

See it in action — watch an intro video

 

What's New - New Functionality That Improves Usability and Reduces App Search Time

New Home Page - A Smarter Starting Point That Gets You to Exactly What You're Looking For
On login, you are redirected to a new home page designed to get you to the right apps fast. A prominent search bar lets you query by keyword, while a Product Suite dropdown lets you scope results to SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, or other environments before you even search.
The Quick Links section puts your most-used release versions one click away — from the latest editions to specific Feature Package Stacks like and Public Cloud releases. A side panel always keeps Home, Apps, Current Selections, and Saved Selections within reach.

 

A smarter starting point — find, filter, and navigate to your apps instantly
Search Wide, Drill Deep — New Filtering Gets You to the Right Apps Fast

In the third quarter of 2024, we released the new List View for the Fiori Apps Library — and the response has been overwhelmingly positive, with great feedback continuing to come in.

The enhanced Apps List View provides a streamlined interface for searching, filtering, and personalizing the display of available apps by product suite and release version. Use this feature to efficiently find, organize, and customize app information to meet your needs.

By default, the List View displays all available apps at their highest release version. Once you select a Product Suite and Release Version, the list instantly narrows to show only what is available in your environment. The horizontal filters at the top address your primary constraints — keyword search, product suite, and release version — for quick and easy navigation.

For more granular exploration, the vertical filter sidebar on the left enables faceted navigation, allowing you to drill down by UI Technology (Fiori, SAP GUI, SAPUI5, WebDynpro), Line of Business (Finance, Supply Chain, Sourcing, etc.), Role, and more.

Start broad, drill deep — layered filters surface the right apps
The two complementary filter layers that work in harmony to help you zero in on exactly the right apps. The horizontal filter bar lets you set your broad selection criteria — such as Product Suite, Release Version, or Line of Business — instantly scoping the entire library to your context (for example, filtering by Finance surfaces 4900 relevant apps). The vertical facet panel then lets you refine further within those results — revealing that 1,014 of those Finance apps also belong specifically to Budget and Finance — without ever losing sight of the broader picture. Together, these two filter levels give you a powerful, layered approach to app discovery: start wide, drill deep, and arrive at a precise, meaningful shortlist in just a few clicks.

All the App Details You Need, Organised So You Can Find Them Without the Effort

The Detail View provides comprehensive information about an app — including its role, product suite, release details, app ID, features, and related apps — organized across several tabs. These cover important SAP Notes, installation requirements (front-end and back-end components), configuration requirements (SAPUI5 applications, OData services, and Fiori Launchpad data), business catalogs, groups, spaces, pages, business roles, as well as extensibility and support information. Together, these details help you assess an app's suitability and plan its implementation effectively.

Everything you need to assess and implement an app — now clearly organized

An Enhanced Selection Experience That Remembers What You're Working On — Every Time

The App Selection feature introduces a clear separation between app discovery and selection, while automatically saving your choices and preserving context throughout your session. It allows you to curate a specific list of applications for implementation, planning, or mass configuration — with selections carried over automatically across user sessions.

You can manage selections individually by using the toggle switch in the Selected column of the apps list view table, or in bulk using the Select All or Deselect All buttons at the top right of the table header. To focus exclusively on your chosen applications, click the Selected (n) tab to filter out all non-selected items.

Selection toggle for app

Current Selections, Always at Hand

Current Selection accessible from side bar, shows a consolidated view of all the apps you have selected across various places within the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library — for example, while browsing the apps list or search results. The key functionality here is the ability to filter this accumulated selection by Product Suite and Release Version, which lets you quickly check whether your chosen apps are available or not available in a specific release.

 

Current Selection shows all your chosen apps — filter by suite and release to confirm availability instantly
Saved Selections - Save Once, Reuse Anytime

Saved Selections also accessible from side bar, allows users to save their current selection under a named list for future reference, making it easy to revisit, load, or share curated sets of apps without having to re-select them each time. A particularly powerful feature visible here is the "Upload App Selection" option in the top right corner, which allows users to import a list of apps directly from a spreadsheet file.

 

Saved selections let you revisit, load, or share curated app sets across sessions
Work in the Language You're Most Comfortable In — Across 10 UI and 40 Content Languages

The library is available in 10 UI languages, configurable via the app or browser settings, with all search and filter categories — including roles, catalogs, and system objects — displayed in your selected language. For localized content, a separate Content Language setting supporting approximately 40 languages allows app metadata such as role names, business catalog titles, and app launcher subtitles to be displayed in your preferred language, making the library accessible and practical for global teams.

Choose your UI language and a separate content language for localized app metadata

What's Coming

Some features, such as Aggregate and Upgrade Analysis, are still making their way to the new version — in the meantime, they remain accessible via the classic library, with seamless navigation between the two. We are committed to continuously enhancing the new library, adding missing functionality and incorporating your feedback along the way.

Getting Started

Navigate to fal.cloud.sap and sign in using your SAP Universal ID, S-User, or P-User. Access to the SAP Fiori Apps Library is restricted to users authenticated via a SAP  Universal ID. If you don't have a Universal ID yet, you can register directly from the start page. Once signed in, your saved selections, view variants, and personalization settings are automatically tied to your account.

Explore and share your feedback.

entry.title: SAP Fiori Development Newsletter issue #40 (May 2026)
entry.content:
The mid-year conference season is upon us with many opportunities for you to meet our development teams and have conversations with your peers about the topics you care about most. Some of our upcoming events include:

  • SAP Sapphire in Orlando on May 11-13
  • SAP UX Innovation Day in Palo Alto on June 11
  • UI5con in St. Leon-Rot on July 14

Join us to learn about using AI to accelerate development, MCP servers, and the latest UX strategy updates from SAP

If you want to join other developers to discuss various traditional and AI-powered Fiori development-related topics from the comfort of your office or home, I encourage you to join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable. Register online or email me for an invitation.

Development News

SAP Fiori elements feature map: what is available in each SAPUI5 version

If you work with SAP Fiori elements, you have probably run into the problem where it is hard to see in which UI5 version a specific feature was introduced. Marian Zeis created a feature map that shows when a feature first appears. It’s updated automatically whenever new versions are released. Explore the feature map.

Key User Adaptation: powerful new features and seamless transition to Developer Extensibility

With key user adaptation, you can change the user interface of SAP Fiori apps directly in the Fiori launchpad. The goal of key user extensibility is to empower business users to be able to adapt their working environment to suit their needs, without having to rely on system administrators. Learn more.

SAP Fiori tools 2603 update: RAP Project Accelerator, walkthrough, adaptation improvements, and more

SAP Fiori tools 2603 delivers improvements across development transparency, onboarding, AI-supported development, and extensibility. Highlights include Project Accelerator support for RAP, a change in navigation from properties to its source, a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code, and the ability to import existing key user changes into an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project. See details.

Introducing UI5 plugins for Claude: AI-powered development for SAPUI5 and OpenUI5

UI5 plugins for Claude enhance UI5 development workflows with AI assistance by embedding current, maintained guidelines directly into the development workflow. The result is that Claude Code provides UI5-accurate suggestions without you having to paste documentation into every conversation. Learn more.

How to migrate UI5 projects to the latest UI5 best practices

This blog post focuses on how UI5 MCP server can support you in migrating your UI5 project easily to the latest best practices with the help of GitHub Copilot Coding Agent. Migrate your project.

Declarative UI Integration Cards - A consistent framework with flexible content

UI Integration Cards are utilized in many SAP solutions. A new blog post shows key qualities of declarative Integration Cards: They offer a range of display options and minimalist interactions, including List Card, Table Card, Timeline, Calendar, Analytical, and Object Card with input elements. Read more.

More power, new name: The UI Flexibility Service explained

SAPUI5 flexibility allows developers, key users, and end users to adapt standard apps with simple drag-and-drop tools. Since it’s not only for key users, we renamed this capability to UI flexibility service instead of the older, potentially confusing name UI5 flexibility for key users. Learn more.

Navigating from an SAP Fiori elements application to another application

While the SAPUI5 documentation explains how to navigate to other applications in detail, the code snippets sometimes make it difficult to visualize how each navigation pattern behaves in the UI. Mio Yasutake created a sample repository that demonstrates various Fiori elements navigation patterns along with their actual UI behavior. See how.

Events

SAP UX Innovation Days are back for 2026

SAP UX Innovation Day Silicon Valley – Thursday, June 11

Join us at SAP UX Innovation Day Silicon Valley to learn about the intersection between AI and user experience. Our goal is to empower you with knowledge on how to build SAP Fiori apps for SAP S/4HANA Cloud and all the new AI functionality that we are building into the SAP Fiori elements framework. We will cover AI for business users and AI-powered tools for application developers.

Register and more details.

Mark your calendar for other SAP UX Innovation Days in your region:

Dublin, Ireland - Wednesday, September 30

Walldorf, Germany - Thursday, December 10

Other Events

Sapphire – lots of great UX sessions

UI5con - A vibrant gathering for UI5 enthusiasts to connect, learn, and innovate

Back issues from the past year

SAP Fiori Development newsletter March 2026 (issue #39)

SAP Fiori development newsletter January 2026 (issue #38)

SAP Fiori development newsletter November 2025 (issue #37)

SAP Fiori development newsletter September 2025 (issue #36)

SAP Fiori development newsletter July 2025 (issue #35)

SAP FIori development newsletter May 2025 (Issue #34)

entry.title: Recovering UI5 adaptation project source from a deployed system
entry.content:
Introduction

UI5 adaptations can keep running in SAP long after the original developer and their local project are gone. This post explains why you cannot treat adaptations like ordinary BSP content, how adaptation files are actually stored, and how we automated downloading them from the layered repository into a local folder. You will learn which database table holds the data, what our small ABAP report does, and what you still need to set up by hand before using the files in your IDE.

The situation

Imagine you are asked to implement additional functionality for an already extended standard UI5 application. The extension is running in the system and users rely on it. The problem is that the team never used proper source code management, and the developer who built the adaptation has left the company—along with the local project files.

So the adaptation exists in SAP and the deployment is there. In theory you only need to “download it” again. In practice this is a bit awkward: there is no single, obvious option comparable to something like /UI5/UI5_REPOSITORY_LOAD that pulls the whole project. A main reason is that these adaptation files are not stored in the BSP repository; they live in the layered repository.

Inspecting files manually

You can inspect adaptation files one by one using the Layered Repository option in transaction SUI_SUPPORT (thanks to @abdullahgunes for pointing this out). 

You can view individual files, download them manually, and rebuild the folder structure yourself. That works, but it becomes tedious as soon as the adaptation has more than a handful of files.

An automated approach

We looked for a more automated way and ended up with a small ABAP report that reads UI5 adaptation files directly from the layered repository and writes them to a local folder on your machine.

The important technical detail: files in the layered repository are persisted in database table /UIF/LREPDCONT. That table holds what you need to reconstruct a project on disk: the namespace (including the project identifier and folder structure), each file’s name and type, and the content in binary form.

The report: ZR_LREP_ADAPTATION_DOWNLOAD

We created report ZR_LREP_ADAPTATION_DOWNLOAD and kept it deliberately simple. It has a single main input: the custom app (variant) ID. You can type it or use value help. When you run the report and choose a target folder, the program downloads all related files and recreates the original folder structure on your local machine.

Limitation: dist folder only

You should be aware of one limitation: at present, only the contents of the dist (which is created as a result of the build step) folder are stored in the layered repository in a way this approach recovers. This is enough to recreate the project as these are same with the contents of the webapp folder. You still need to create a dummy UI5 adaptation project in your IDE and then move the downloaded  content to the webapp folder into that project so you do not miss files such as ui5.yaml & package.json.

Reference implementation

The full source for the report is available here:

https://github.com/onurakca/SAPUI5-Adaptation-Project-Downloader

The report is provided free of charge under the MIT License. Use it at your own risk: test in non-production systems first, verify behavior in your landscape, and ensure compliance with your organization’s rules for custom ABAP and repository access.

Conclusion

This post walked through how UI5 adaptations are tied to the layered repository rather than classic BSP download flows, how table /UIF/LREPDCONT stores the files you need, and how ZR_LREP_ADAPTATION_DOWNLOAD bulk-exports them while preserving project structure. You now know the gap between “deployment exists in SAP” and “IDE-ready project,” including the webapp-only limitation and the step of dropping files into a new adaptation shell. Even with that limitation, this can save a lot of time when you inherit an adaptation with no Git history and need to review, compare, or extend it quickly.

entry.title: New Practice System for Learning the Basics of SAP Fiori (UX100)
entry.content:
Last year, we took the practice system for the Learning the Basics of SAP Fiori (UX100) course offline to upgrade its capabilities to better reflect the latest additions to the course material. This was right before our latest innovation was kicked off internally: SAP Certifications reimagined.

Unfortunately, this shift of priorities led to some delay for this practice system. But now it is here: SAP Fiori – Foundation

  • You get full administrative access to an SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS04 like you would get in the classroom version of the UX100.
  • You get full administrative access to an SAP BTP subaccount with entitlements for SAP Business Application Studio and SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.

All exercises can be done without any limitation:

  • Explore a fresh installed SAP S/4HANA with SAP Best Practices in place.
  • Use the fully configured SAP Fiori launchpad.
  • Set up SAP Fiori spaces, pages, and catalogs.
  • Dive into SAP Fiori development and system administration.

It is all here, just one booking away. Are you ready for some exercise(s)?

💡Hint:
UX100 is part of the learning journeys Developing SAPUI5 Applications and SAP Fiori – System Administration, which both lead to a certification. Check-out my blog post summarizing UX100.

entry.title: How I transformed my SAPUI5 development from code to visual
entry.content:
Introduction

Ever wondered if there's some way to draw SAPUI5 freestyle screens? Well, that was possible back in the days and I miss it. And because of that, I decided to make it possible again.

 

The matter in hand

I've been working with SAPUI5 since 2020, developing freestyle apps in the SAP WEB IDE (good old days). I started when Fiori Elements wasn't as mature as it is today, with just SEGW as a tool to create oData services.

Nowadays we have Visual Studio Code, and it's great! It's lightweight and has so many advantages compared to the early Fiori development tools. But I always felt that some things didn't evolve like I wanted to. I feel some features are missing, like the visual editor:

 

Ok, it's true it's better to create the view using XML code. But everyone should remember many Fiori developers are also ABAP developers. And honestly, ABAP developers are not usually very UI-focused. We loved Module Pool (kinda).

So, I decided to create something to help me out and thought "Why not everyone else". With this, I built a small open-source tool to explore this idea. It originated standalone as a web app, but now it's fully integrated with Vscode.

The idea is simple. Design a screen with drag-and-drop:

Generate an XML:

And also save as file inside the project. 

 

The reverse is also possible: open an existing view with the visual editor:

Also, one thing that I emphasized is the properties inspector. You can edit properties from a component visually:

There are some presets when you want a quick boilerplate:

 

And then we can test it with the preview application:

Sure, it's flawed yet. The extension is in active development, but there are a lot of features that can help the day-to-day development:

  • Open a visual UI5 screen designer directly inside VS Code.
  • Build SAPUI5/OpenUI5 screen wireframes using visual components.
  • Add components by clicking or dragging them onto the canvas.
  • Move, resize, duplicate, copy, paste, and delete components.
  • Select and manipulate multiple components at once.
  • Edit component properties through a visual inspector.
  • Generate XML View code automatically from the visual layout.
  • Copy the generated XML to the clipboard.
  • Save the XML as a new file inside the workspace.
  • Open an existing XML View from the VS Code context menu.
  • Preserve or infer the View controllerName when generating XML.
  • Generate responsive layouts, avoiding fixed pixel widths where possible.
  • Use UI5 layout patterns such as VBox, HBox, SimpleForm, FlexItemData, percentages, and rem spacing.
  • Automatically detect required namespaces such as f, core, uxap, and unified.
  • Preserve common bindings when importing and regenerating XML, including table bindings.
  • Suggest bindings based on local OData metadata when a manifest.json and metadata file are available.
  • Run the whole generation flow locally, without sending XML or wireframes to external services.
  • Create empty UI5 view with corresponding controller with just 1 click, automatically.

The extension is still in preview/development. Generated XML should be reviewed before being used in production, especially for complex or highly customized UI5 Views.

The extension is called UI5 Designer 

And also, the project is open source on GitHub 

 

Conclusion

So that's that. The project is still on development, but for me that's something I could use already. I hope it can help everyone designing, prototyping and maintaining SAPUI5 Views easier and visually.

I'd love to hear from everyone, and also improve it for the community.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-1]
entry.content:
Developing the CAP Application Locally with SQLite

In this blog series, I will walk you through building an Incident Management application using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) with Node.js, connected to SAP HANA Cloud, and deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment.

The frontend is a Fiori Elements application deployed separately to the HTML5 Application Repository — making this a fully decoupled architecture.

Here is what we will cover across this series:

— Develop the CAP application locally using SQLite (this post)
— Connect the CAP app to SAP HANA Cloud
— Deploy the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry
— Build the Fiori Elements UI using SAP Fiori tools
— Configuring Destination in BTP
— Deploy the Fiori app to the HTML5 Application Repository

Before deploying anything to the cloud, it is always a good practice to build and test your CAP application locally. In this part, we will:

- Initialize the CAP project
- Define the domain model (entities)
- Create two OData services (Admin and Processor)
- Add sample data using CSV files
- Run and test the application locally using SQLite as the database

Let's get started!


PREREQUISITES

Make sure the following tools are installed and configured on your machine before proceeding:

- Node.js (v18 or higher recommended)
- @sap/cds-dk installed globally:

npm install -g @sap/cds-dk

- VS Code with the SAP CDS Language Support extension (recommended) / Business Application Studio (BAS)
- Basic familiarity with CAP and OData concepts is helpful


STEP 1 — CREATE THE CAP PROJECT

Open a terminal and run the following command to initialize a new CAP project with Node.js support:

cds init --add nodejs incident-management

This creates a new folder called "incident-management" with the standard CAP project structure.

Navigate into the project folder:

cd incident-management

Install the required Node.js dependencies:

npm install

 

After installation, your project structure will look like this:

incident-management/
├── db/ → Domain models (entities, types)
├── srv/ → Service definitions (OData services)
├── app/ → UI applications (used in later parts)
├── package.json
└── .cdsrc.json


STEP 2 — ADD THE DOMAIN MODEL

The domain model defines all the data entities for our application.
Inside the "db" folder, create a new file named: schema.cds

Paste the following code into schema.cds:

using {
  cuid,
  managed,
  sap.common.CodeList
} from '@sap/cds/common';

namespace sap.capire.incidents;

/**
* Incidents created by Customers.
*/
entity Incidents : cuid, managed {
  customer     : Association to Customers;
  title        : String @title: 'Title';
  urgency      : Association to Urgency default 'M';
  status       : Association to Status default 'N';
  conversation : Composition of many {
                   key ID        : UUID;
                       timestamp : type of managed : createdAt;
                       author    : type of managed : createdBy;
                       message   : String;
                 };
}

/**
* Customers entitled to create support Incidents.
*/
entity Customers : managed {
  key ID           : String;
      firstName    : String;
      lastName     : String;
      name         : String = trim(firstName || ' ' || lastName);
      email        : EMailAddress;
      phone        : PhoneNumber;
      incidents    : Association to many Incidents
                       on incidents.customer = $self;
      creditCardNo : String(16) @assert.format: '^[1-9]\d{15}$';
      addresses    : Composition of many Addresses
                       on addresses.customer = $self;
}

entity Addresses : cuid, managed {
  customer      : Association to Customers;
  city          : String;
  postCode      : String;
  streetAddress : String;
}

entity Status : CodeList {
  key code        : String enum {
        new = 'N';
        assigned = 'A';
        in_process = 'I';
        on_hold = 'H';
        resolved = 'R';
        closed = 'C';
      };
      criticality : Integer;
}

entity Urgency : CodeList {
  key code        : String enum {
        high = 'H';
        medium = 'M';
        low = 'L';
      };
      criticality : Integer;
}

type EMailAddress : String;
type PhoneNumber  : String;

The model uses the namespace: sap.capire.incidents

It imports three built-in CDS types from @sap/cds/common:

- cuid → automatically generates a UUID as the primary key.
- managed → automatically tracks createdAt, createdBy, modifiedAt, modifiedBy.
- CodeList → a base type for value-list entities like Status and Urgency.

Here is a description of each entity defined in the schema:

INCIDENTS
The core entity of the application. Each incident is raised by a Customer and has a title, an urgency level (default: Medium), and a status (default: New). It also contains an inline Composition of conversations — meaning each incident can have multiple conversation/comment entries stored together with it.

CUSTOMERS
Represents the customers who raise incidents. Stores their first name, last name, email, phone, and credit card number. The "name" field is a computed field that automatically combines firstName and lastName. Each customer can have multiple Addresses and multiple Incidents linked to them.

ADDRESSES
Stores the physical address of a customer, including city, postal code, and street address. It is a composition child of Customers.

STATUS
A code list entity representing the lifecycle stages of an incident:
N → New
A → Assigned
I → In Process
H → On Hold
R → Resolved
C → Closed
Each status has a criticality value used for UI color coding in Fiori.

URGENCY
A code list entity representing the priority level of an incident:
H → High
M → Medium
L → Low
Each urgency level also carries a criticality value for UI color coding.


STEP 3 — CREATE THE SERVICES

CAP uses service definitions to expose your entities as OData endpoints.
We will create two separate service files inside the "srv" folder.

a) incidents_admin.cds

This service is used by administrators to manage customers and incidents. It exposes the Customers entity (with OData Draft enabled for edit flows) and the Incidents entity.

The service is published at the path: /odata/v4/incidents-admin

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../db/schema';


/**
 * Service used by administrators to manage customers and incidents.
 */

 : 'incidents-admin'
service AdminService {
    .draft.enabled
    entity Customers as projection on my.Customers;
    entity Incidents as projection on my.Incidents;
}


b) incidents_processor.cds

This service is used by support personnel (incident processors). It exposes the Incidents entity (with OData Draft enabled) as the main working entity. The Customers entity is auto-exposed as a read-only entity — meaning processors can view customer data when working on an incident, but cannot modify it.

The service is published at the path: /odata/v4/incidents-processor

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../db/schema';

/**
 * Service used by support personell, i.e. the incidents' 'processors'.
 */


@path: 'incidents-processor'
service ProcessorService  {
    .draft.enabled
    entity Incidents as projection on my.Incidents;

    // 
    // entity Customers as projection on my.Customers;

    annotate my.Customers with .autoexpose; // Exposes Customers entity as read only entity
}


> WHY TWO SERVICES?
Separating admin and processor services is a best practice in CAP. It lets you assign different authorization roles to each service and expose only what each user role actually needs. This follows the  principle of least privilege and makes it easier to apply security restrictions later (covered in a future part of this series).


STEP 4 — GENERATE CSV TEMPLATES AND ADD INITIAL DATA

CAP provides a built-in command to auto-generate CSV template files based on your domain model.

Run:

cds add data

This creates empty CSV files inside a newly created "db/data" folder.

The file names follow the pattern: <namespace>-<EntityName>.csv
e.g., sap.capire.incidents-Customers.csv

CAP automatically picks up these files and loads them as seed/initial data every time the application starts in development mode.

Now add the following sample data to each file:

a) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Addresses.csv
Contains address records for each customer (city, postal code, street).

ID,customer_ID,city,postCode,streetAddress
17e00347-dc7e-4ca9-9c5d-06ccef69f064,1004155,Rome,00164,Piazza Adriana
d8e797d9-6507-4aaa-b43f-5d2301df5135,1004161,Munich,80809,Olympia Park
ff13d2fa-e00f-4ee5-951c-3303f490777b,1004100,Walldorf,69190,Dietmar-Hopp-Allee


b) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Customers.csv
Contains three sample customers with ID, name, email, and phone.

ID,firstName,lastName,email,phone
1004155,Daniel,Watts,daniel.watts@demo.com,+39-555-123
1004161,Stormy,Weathers,stormy.weathers@demo.com,+49-020-022
1004100,Sunny,Sunshine,sunny.sunshine@demo.com,+49-555-789


c) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Incidents.conversation.csv
Contains conversation/comment entries linked to incidents via the
"up__ID" foreign key (CAP convention for composition parent).

ID,up__ID,timestamp,author,message
2b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,3b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,1995-12-17T03:24:00Z,Harry John,Can you please check if battery connections are fine?
2b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd843c,3a4ede72-244a-4f5f-8efa-b17e032d01ee,1995-12-18T04:24:00Z,Emily Elizabeth,Can you please check if there are any loose connections?
9583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,3583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,2022-09-04T12:00:00Z,Sunny Sunshine,Please check why the solar panel is broken.
9583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a158cd7,3ccf474c-3881-44b7-99fb-59a2a4668418,2022-09-04T13:00:00Z,Bradley Flowers,What exactly is wrong?


d) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Incidents.csv
Contains four sample incidents with title, urgency code, status code, and a reference to the customer who raised them.

ID,customer_ID,title,urgency_code,status_code
3b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,1004155,Inverter not functional,H,C
3a4ede72-244a-4f5f-8efa-b17e032d01ee,1004161,No current on a sunny day,H,N
3ccf474c-3881-44b7-99fb-59a2a4668418,1004161,Strange noise when switching off Inverter,M,N
3583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,1004100,Solar panel broken,H,I


e) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Status.csv
Contains the status code list: N, A, I, H, R, C with descriptions and criticality values.

code,descr,criticality
N,New,0
A,Assigned,0
I,In Process,2
H,On Hold,0
R,Resolved,3
C,Closed,1


f) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Urgency.csv
Contains the urgency code list: H, M, L with descriptions and criticality values.

code,descr,criticality
H,High,1
M,Medium,2
L,Low,3


STEP 5 — RUN THE PROJECT WITH SQLITE

First, deploy the schema to a local SQLite database file:

cds deploy --to sqlite

Then start the development server with live-reload:

cds watch

Once the server starts, open your browser and go to: http://localhost:4004 in your browser.

You should see the CDS welcome page listing all available OData service endpoints:

 

The two services and their exposed entities will be listed:

/odata/v4/incidents-admin
→ Customers (with Fiori preview)
→ Incidents (with Fiori preview)

/odata/v4/incidents-processor
→ Incidents (with Fiori preview)
→ Customers (with Fiori preview — read-only)

You can click the "Fiori preview" link next to any entity to open an auto-generated Fiori Elements UI — useful for quick testing without building a full UI yet.

You can also directly access the raw OData feed in the browser.

For example: http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents

This will return the incident records from your CSV seed data in JSON format, confirming that the data was loaded correctly.

 


SUMMARY

In Part 1, we successfully:

✔ Created a CAP Node.js project using cds init
✔ Defined the domain model with Incidents, Customers, Addresses, Conversations, Status, and Urgency entities
✔ Created two OData services — AdminService and ProcessorService —with appropriate entity exposure for each user role
✔ Added initial seed data using CSV files
✔ Ran and validated the application locally using SQLite

The application is fully functional locally. In Part 2 , we will replace SQLite with SAP HANA Cloud and prepare the project for cloud deployment.

entry.title: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - What’s New in Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment [V3.0]
entry.content:
SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – What’s New in the 2026 Update of Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment

As you may already know, SAP continues to recommend deploying SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA using an embedded SAP Fiori Front-End Server.

Over the years, many customers who initially started with a standalone FES have been transitioning to embedded deployment to reduce complexity, optimise operations, and align with SAP’s long term architecture.
Looking ahead, this direction becomes even more explicit. As outlined in the SAP Fiori Deployment Options and System Landscape Recommendations  (as of Guide’s Version 10.3, February 2026) under the section Outlook: Fiori Deployment, the guide explicitly states:

SAP Fiori Deployment Options and System Landscape Recommendations, Section 'Outlook: Fiori Deployment''

“Please be aware that as of SAP S/4HANA 2027 the embedded deployment option will be the only available deployment option”. This effectively makes embedded deployment not just the recommended approach, but the standard going forward for SAP Fiori in SAP S/4HANA landscapes.

In this context, we’re excited to share the updated “Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA”  guide, based on SAP S/4HANA 2025, which becomes even more relevant for customers planning or progressing through this transition.

The guide was first published in October 2019 (version 1.0). It has since evolved through multiple updates, including version 2.1 in December 2024, which focused on SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 2023. The most recent version, 3.0 released in March 2026, is based on SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 2025.

Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA (V3.0)

 This latest update (Version 3.0, March 2026) has been driven with strong input from SAP product experts, including Peter Persiel (peterpersiel) , whose contributions have helped shape the guide based on real-world customer experiences and evolving SAP Fiori architecture.

So what actually changed?

This blog highlights the latest updates and key enhancements introduced in the 2026 version of the guide (Version 3.0 March 2026), helping you quickly understand what’s new, what’s improved, and what to consider for your projects going forward.

Why this update matters?

Version 3.0 of this guide is an important refresh. It reflects:

  • Alignment with SAP S/4HANA 2025 and SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 2025, and important announcements planned in the future releases of SAP S/4HANA.
  • The continued evolution of SAP Fiori architecture and activation tooling, especially in embedded deployments
  • Lessons learned from more recent customer implementations, translating real project experience into more practical guidance
  • Increased focus on clean core, maintainability, and lifecycle management

Why do you need this guide?

This guide is essential for SAP S/4HANA customers currently running or considering a hub/standalone SAP Fiori Front-End Server deployment, and who are planning or preparing for a transition from a hub/standalone SAP Fiori Front-End Server deployment to an embedded deployment.

It provides clear, structured guidance to support a smooth transition, covering both technical and practical aspects of the move. Whether you are upgrading your SAP S/4HANA system or proactively aligning with SAP’s long term architectural direction, this guide helps you plan and execute the transition with confidence.

From planning, installation, and configuration to post migration validation and optimisation, it brings together everything you need to successfully move to an embedded SAP Fiori deployment.

It helps you:

  • Understand the benefits of moving to embedded FES deployment
  • Know the right time to make the shift
  • Navigate technical prerequisites, sizing, and system considerations
  • Simplify the migration process with actionable steps, tools, and examples
  • Ensure a seamless experience for users by resolving common issues
  • Minimise disruptions by retaining user personalisation and role assignments
  • Gain tips on how to enhance usability and performance

By following this guide, you can align with SAP best practices and unlock the full potential of SAP Fiori embedded deployment.

What's in it / What's new?

  • Simplified architecture in SAP S/4HANA 2025
    Some Front-end components are now part of the core platform, reducing system complexity
  • More practical, end-to-end guidance
    Covers not just migration steps, but also planning, validation, and long term operations
  • Better control of custom content migration
    Helps define what custom content to move and adds validation after migration instead of blindly transporting everything
  • Improved transport strategy guidance
    Real world options for handling transports depending on your landscape and ongoing development activities
  • Extended launchpad configuration coverage
    Includes transport of launchpad parameters, plug ins, and configuration settings beyond just apps and catalogs
  • Built-in validation step
    Dedicated step to validate custom content in the embedded system after migration
  • Enhanced personalisation migration
    Clear guidance using tools like /UIF/MIGRATE_FES_PERSO to retain user settings, homepages, and UI changes
  • Explicit system alias and RFC adjustments
    Clear steps to adjust system aliases and RFC destinations post migration
  • Smarter SAP Notes handling
    Explicit step to identify and implement missing SAP Notes after migration, avoiding common post go live issues
  • Stronger focus on business roles
    Additional step to review and optimise business roles after migration in line with modern Fiori design

Becoming a SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guru

You’ll find much more on our  SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA topic page

Other helpful links:

  • SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – Foundational SAP Notes for SAP S/4HANA 2025
  • See all questions and answers about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 
  • Follow SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates 
  • Ask a Question about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG and Customer Care team.

entry.title: Accelerating SAP Fiori & CAP Development With SAP MCP Server + GitHub Copilot in VS Code
entry.content:
☕The 5-Hour CAP App That Now Takes 30 Minutes

Last month, I sat down with one of our experienced SAP CAP developers and asked her to build a small Fiori Elements app on top of a CAP Java service — start to first working screen. She'd done dozens of these. It took her just under five hours.

A week later, I asked a colleague — a senior ABAP developer who'd never written a line of CDS or Spring Boot — to build the same thing using GitHub Copilot Chat plus the new SAP MCP Server in VS Code.

He shipped a working app — CAP service with one entity, Fiori Elements List Report + Object Page, draft handling, basic security — in 38 minutes

That gap isn't a fluke. It's the new reality for SAP development — and in this post I want to walk you through what changed, how the pieces fit together, and how to set this up yourself.

🎯Why This Matters Right Now

If you build on SAP, you've felt the friction:

  • 🐌Annotations are unforgiving. A single misplaced UI.LineItem token can swallow an afternoon.
  • 📚Documentation is fragmented across cap.cloud.sap, help.sap.com, blogs, and Discovery Center.
  • ☕CAP Java handlers require Spring Boot fluency that ABAP developers don't have on day one.
  • 🔌BTP wiring — mta.yaml, xs-security.json, destinations, role collections — is unforgiving and project-after-project repetitive.

That's exactly what changed in the last few months.

🧩 The Three Pieces That Snap Together

The breakthrough isn't any one tool — it's three components working as a system.

1️⃣Visual Studio Code
